text: The robotic instruments retract the left lobe of the liver, and liver retraction is achieved using the Dima Havaleshko liver suspension stitch.
Arm three is equipped with a needle driver or vessel sealer in preparation for the dissection phase of the robotic sleeve gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a needle driver to secure the suture to the anterior abdominal wall, while preparing to exchange the instrument for a vessel sealer.
The surgeon uses the Cartier instrument to measure six centimeters from the pylorus, marking the location with the vessel sealer.
The surgeon uses the vessel sealer to detach the stomach from the gastroepiploic vessels, carefully severing the attachments between these structures.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the stomach, working proximally to avoid thermal injury before progressing cephalad.
Arms one and four of the robotic system retract, providing better exposure of the surgical field.
The surgical arm equipped with a vessel sealer detaches the attachments along the greater curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon elevates the fundus of the stomach and uses a vessel sealer to detach the posterior attachments adjacent to the spleen, pancreas, and fat pad.
The surgeon retracts the stomach cephalad and uses a vessel sealer to dissect and clear the attachments to the left cruciate of the diaphragm, removing the anterior fat pad.
With the 36 French bougie in place, the surgeon continues to dissect the fat pad in a medial direction using surgical instruments.
The surgeon retracts the fundus laterally, facilitating the dissection of the fat pad in a medial direction towards the margin of the bougie, preparing for the eventual passing of the stapler.
The surgeon uses a liver suture retractor to hold the fat pad, keeping it clear of the surgical field.
The surgeon takes down additional posterior fat pad tissue to ensure the stomach has a uniform thickness suitable for stapler passage.
The bougie is visibly advanced down to the antrum of the stomach, guided carefully along the gastric lumen.
The surgeon dissects and removes posterior attachments of the stomach, ensuring clear access for the stapler to pass along the margin of the bougie.
The surgical camera is repositioned to arm three, while arm two is equipped with a twelve millimeter port designated for the stapler insertion.
The surgeon uses a green load stapler to divide the antrum of the stomach at a pre-marked point near the pylorus.
The surgeon carefully maintains an adequate distance from the incisura angularis while preparing to divide the stomach, ensuring the avoidance of an hourglass deformity.

The bougie is aligned along the lesser curvature of the stomach, ensuring adequate distance from the incisura angularis while resecting the fundus proximally. The surgeon measures the distance from the incisura both anteriorly and posteriorly to minimize postoperative nausea.
The surgeon divides the stomach at the pre-marked point, and excess staples are removed from the surgical site.
The surgeon reloads the stapler, preparing to continue the division of the stomach.

The surgeon uses the third fire of the bluewood stapler, aligning it parallel to the bougie, to continue resecting the stomach in a cephalad direction. The surgeon checks both anterior and posterior dimensions to confirm the appropriate amount of stomach tissue has been resected.
Arm four retracts the fundus of the stomach, creating space for the stapler to be positioned for the final application.

The surgeon equalizes the anterior and posterior dimensions of the stomach, carefully checking the distance from the gastroesophageal junction both posteriorly and anteriorly to confirm it remains unencroached.
The surgeon checks the position of the esophagus and the gastroesophageal junction before clamping and firing the stapler to complete the resection.
The surgeon completes the division of the stomach, separating the specimen from the sleeve stomach, with the stapler visibly marking the line of division.
The surgeon performs transsection using the cold scissors function of the Vessel sealer, cutting through the stomach tissue.
With the specimen removed, the surgeon introduces a 3-0 PDS suture on an SH-1 needle into the abdomen to begin oversewing the sleeve staple line.
The surgeon dunks the proximal corner of the sleeve stomach to initiate the imbrication process, ensuring the tissue is folded and secured.
The surgeon continues suturing caudally along the sleeve staple line, using the back of the needle driver to draw the suture through the tissue.
The surgeon approximates the serosa of the anterior and posterior stomach to imbricate the staple line, ensuring it is not visible by the end of the operation.
The surgeon removes a staple that was obstructing proper imbrication of the gastric tissue.
The surgeon carefully sutures around large vessels near the sleeve staple line, ensuring they are not injured during the oversewing process.
The surgeon carefully sutures beneath a large vessel, ensuring it is not injured during the procedure.
The surgeon uses the suture and the back of the needle driver to facilitate the tightening of sutures along the staple line, ensuring smooth approximation of the serosa.
The surgeon manually adjusts the corner sutures to facilitate inward dunking, enhancing the imbrication of the staple line on the stomach.

In the distal antrum, the surgeon performs an omental gastropexy, securing the omentum to weigh down the gastric sleeve in a smooth, laminar manner. This maneuver also pulls open the incisura angularis to prevent twisting, kinking, or narrowing.
The surgeon advances the running suture to the bottom of the staple line and tightens it, ensuring secure closure along the surgical site.
The surgeon runs the suture back upon itself twice for secure closure before cutting it with the scissors function of the vessel sealer and removing it from the abdomen.
The final appearance of the sleeve gastrectomy is shown, displaying a smooth, tapered shape without any visible kinking or narrowing.
The surgical table is flattened, and an endoscopic leak test is performed, showing no extravasation of carbon dioxide from the sleeve gastrectomy.
